meta.title: AWS-DEA-1: Kinesis, Glue, Redshift and Lake Formation

questions.stem: A company needs to ingest clickstream data from a website at 100,000 events per second with sub-second latency for real-time analytics. Which AWS service is best suited for this ingestion layer?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is designed for real-time, high-throughput data streaming at massive scale (up to millions of records per second). It provides sub-second latency and enables multiple consumers to process the same stream simultaneously.

options.text: Amazon SQS
options.explanation: is incorrect. SQS is a message queue suitable for decoupling services but is not designed for high-throughput real-time streaming analytics — it lacks the ordered, replay-capable, multi-consumer semantics Kinesis provides.

options.text: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)
options.explanation: is incorrect. DMS is for database migration and replication, not high-throughput streaming event ingestion.

options.text: Amazon S3 with event notifications
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 event notifications trigger Lambda or SQS on object PUT events but cannot handle 100K events/second with sub-second latency as an ingestion layer.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon Kinesis Data Streams? - Amazon Kinesis Data StreamsWhat is Amazon Kinesis Data Streams? - Amazon Kinesis Data Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue ETL job fails with OutOfMemoryError when processing a 500 GB Parquet file from S3. The job uses DynamicFrames. What is the most effective solution?

options.text: Enable job bookmarks to skip already-processed records
options.explanation: is incorrect. Job bookmarks skip previously processed data and are useful for incremental loads, but they do not address an OutOfMemoryError.

options.text: Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue scales by allocating more DPUs (Data Processing Units). Each DPU provides 4 vCPUs and 16 GB memory. Increasing DPUs provides more total memory and parallelism to handle the 500 GB file, distributing the data across more executor nodes.

options.text: Switch from DynamicFrames to RDD operations
options.explanation: is incorrect. Switching from DynamicFrames to RDD operations does not inherently solve memory issues — both run on the same Spark engine and consume similar memory.

options.text: Use Glue Streaming instead of batch processing
options.explanation: is incorrect. Glue Streaming is for continuous ingestion of live data streams, not for processing a single large batch Parquet file.

questions.reference: [AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html)

questions.stem: You need to consume streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams, apply optional Lambda-based transformations, and load the results into Amazon Redshift with Amazon S3 as the staging layer. Which managed AWS service is best suited for this delivery layer?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose)
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Data Firehose is the managed delivery service purpose-built for streaming data delivery. It can read from Kinesis Data Streams, invoke Lambda for record transformation, buffer data reliably, land data in Amazon S3, and load Amazon Redshift by using S3 as the intermediate staging layer.

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics
options.explanation: is incorrect. Kinesis Data Analytics (Managed Service for Apache Flink) is an analytics engine for stateful stream processing, not the managed delivery layer for loading Redshift from Kinesis. You would still need additional sink configuration and delivery plumbing.

options.text: AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Lambda consumer could write to both Amazon Redshift and Amazon S3, but that approach requires custom fan-out, buffering, retry, and backpressure handling that Firehose provides as a managed capability.

options.text: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon MSK is a managed Kafka service, not the delivery service for this Kinesis-based requirement, and it still needs additional consumers or connectors to write to Redshift and S3.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon Data Firehose?](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/what-is-this-service.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job reads from a JDBC source and writes to S3 in Parquet format. After several months, you notice that the number of output Parquet files is very large (500,000+ small files). How should you reduce the file count?

options.text: Enable Glue job bookmarks to consolidate output files
options.explanation: is incorrect. Job bookmarks track which data has been processed to avoid reprocessing; they do not consolidate output files.

options.text: Call coalesce() or repartition() on the DynamicFrame before writing to reduce the number of Spark partitions
options.explanation: is correct. Glue DynamicFrames are backed by Spark DataFrames. Using `.toDF().coalesce(N)` (or `.repartition(N)`) reduces the number of Spark output partitions before writing, directly controlling the number of output files. For example, `coalesce(100)` outputs approximately 100 Parquet files.

options.text: Use the Glue merge operation to compact files post-write
options.explanation: is incorrect. Glue merge is used for merging Iceberg/Delta/Hudi tables with upsert semantics, not for compacting plain Parquet output files.

options.text: Enable S3 multipart upload to combine files during write
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 multipart upload is a mechanism for efficiently uploading individual large files; it does not combine multiple small output files into one.

questions.reference: [Managing partitions for ETL output in AWS Glue - AWS GlueManaging partitions for ETL output in AWS Glue - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-partitions.html)

questions.stem: You need to replicate changes from an on-premises Oracle database to Amazon S3 in near real-time using change data capture (CDC). Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled
options.explanation: is correct. AWS DMS supports CDC by reading the database transaction log (Oracle redo log) and replicating INSERTs, UPDATEs, and DELETEs to a target. DMS can write CDC events directly to S3 in CSV or Parquet format with near real-time latency.

options.text: AWS DataSync
options.explanation: is incorrect. DataSync is for bulk file/object storage migration (copying files from NFS, SMB, or HDFS to S3) — it does not perform database CDC.

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a custom Oracle listener
options.explanation: is incorrect. Building a custom Oracle listener and publishing to Kinesis requires significant custom engineering and maintenance; DMS is the purpose-built service.

options.text: AWS Glue with JDBC connection polling every 5 minutes
options.explanation: is incorrect. JDBC polling is not CDC — it misses deletes, cannot detect updates without a timestamp column, runs in batch intervals, and does not provide near real-time replication.

questions.reference: [What is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration ServiceWhat is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dms/latest/userguide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: Which AWS service provides a serverless SQL interface to query data directly in Amazon S3 without loading it into a database?

options.text: Amazon Athena
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Athena is a serverless, interactive query service that uses standard SQL to analyze data directly in Amazon S3. It requires no ETL or data loading — you define a schema via AWS Glue Data Catalog and query immediately. You pay per query based on data scanned.

options.text: Amazon Redshift
options.explanation: is incorrect. Redshift requires data to be loaded into the cluster's local storage (though Redshift Spectrum can also query S3 as an add-on).

options.text: AWS Glue DataBrew
options.explanation: is incorrect. Glue DataBrew is a visual data preparation tool for cleaning and normalizing data — it is not a SQL query engine for ad-hoc S3 analysis.

options.text: Amazon EMR
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon EMR can run Spark/Hive/Presto to query S3, but it requires cluster provisioning and management — it is not serverless.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon Athena? - Amazon AthenaWhat is Amazon Athena? - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/what-is.html)

questions.stem: A data engineering team uses AWS Glue to transform data. They want to implement unit testing for Glue ETL scripts locally before deploying to AWS. Which TWO approaches support local testing of Glue scripts? (Select TWO)

options.text: AWS Glue Interactive Sessions in a Jupyter notebook
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue Interactive Sessions provide a notebook-based development experience connected to a serverless Glue session, enabling iterative development and testing without a full job run.

options.text: AWS Glue local development libraries (aws-glue-libs) with Docker
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue provides `aws-glue-libs` as a Docker image for local development — you can run and test Glue PySpark scripts on a local machine without connecting to AWS.

options.text: Testing directly in the AWS Glue console with live production data
options.explanation: is incorrect. Testing in the production console with live production data is a bad practice that risks corrupting production data and incurring unexpected costs.

options.text: Amazon EMR Serverless with Glue job templates
options.explanation: is incorrect. EMR Serverless is a separate service and does not support running Glue scripts natively — Glue and EMR Serverless use different runtime environments.

questions.reference: [Developing and testing AWS Glue job scripts locally - AWS GlueDeveloping and testing AWS Glue job scripts locally - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-libraries.html)

questions.stem: You are using Amazon Data Firehose to deliver JSON records to S3. You want records to be automatically converted to Parquet and partitioned by year/month/day without writing custom code. Which Firehose feature provides this?

options.text: Firehose dynamic partitioning with data format conversion (Apache Parquet via AWS Glue schema)
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Data Firehose supports native data format conversion (JSON to Parquet or ORC) using a schema defined in AWS Glue Data Catalog. Dynamic partitioning allows extracting key values from JSON records and using them to partition S3 output (e.g., `year=2024/month=01/day=15`). Both features are built-in to Firehose, requiring no Lambda functions or post-processing jobs.

options.text: An AWS Lambda transformation function that converts JSON to Parquet before delivery
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Lambda transformation function can modify record content (e.g., filter or enrich), but it cannot natively convert JSON to columnar Parquet format — Firehose's built-in format conversion does this without Lambda.

options.text: An S3 lifecycle policy that converts JSON files to Parquet after delivery
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 lifecycle policies manage object transitions (Standard to Glacier) and expiration, not format conversion.

options.text: AWS Glue ETL job triggered by S3 event notification after Firehose delivery
options.explanation: is incorrect. Triggering a Glue ETL job after delivery adds latency and operational overhead — Firehose's built-in conversion eliminates the need for a separate job.

questions.reference: [Convert input data format in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data FirehoseConvert input data format in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/record-format-conversion.html)

questions.stem: Your data lake on S3 has grown to 5 PB with files from multiple years. Most queries access only the last 90 days of data. How should you configure S3 to minimize storage costs for older data?

options.text: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all objects
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 Intelligent-Tiering also saves costs for data with unknown or changing access patterns, but it charges per-object monitoring fees which can be significant at PB scale. Lifecycle policies are more cost-effective when access patterns are predictable.

options.text: Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year
options.explanation: is correct. S3 lifecycle policies automatically transition objects to lower-cost storage classes based on age. Transitioning to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval (millisecond retrieval for occasional access) after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive (lowest cost, hours retrieval) after 1 year provides significant cost savings for rarely accessed historical data while keeping recent data in S3 Standard for fast queries.

options.text: Delete objects older than 1 year
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deleting older data destroys it permanently — this is only appropriate if retention is not required.

options.text: Enable S3 Requester Pays for older data
options.explanation: is incorrect. Requester Pays shifts the cost of data access to the requester but does not reduce storage costs.

questions.reference: [Managing the lifecycle of objects - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceManaging the lifecycle of objects - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift cluster experiences slow query performance on a table with 500 million rows. EXPLAIN plan shows data redistribution between nodes. The table is queried with joins on customer_id. What should you do?

options.text: Set the distribution style to ALL for the table
options.explanation: is incorrect. DISTSTYLE ALL copies the entire table to every node, which is only suitable for small dimension tables (typically under 1 GB). For a 500 million row table this would waste enormous storage.

options.text: Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node
options.explanation: is correct. In Amazon Redshift, when two large tables are joined and do not have matching distribution keys, Redshift must redistribute (move) data across nodes — a costly network operation. Setting DISTKEY to `customer_id` on both tables ensures rows with the same `customer_id` are co-located on the same node slice, eliminating redistribution.

options.text: Change the sort key to customer_id
options.explanation: is incorrect. Sort keys improve scan efficiency by enabling zone map-based block skipping, but they do not affect how data is distributed across nodes and therefore do not fix redistribution.

options.text: Enable result caching for all queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result caching returns cached query results for identical subsequent queries, but it does not fix the underlying slow join caused by redistribution.

questions.reference: [Distribution styles - Amazon RedshiftDistribution styles - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_choosing_dist_sort.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement a data lakehouse on AWS with support for ACID transactions, time travel queries, and schema evolution on S3-backed tables. Which table format should you use?

options.text: Plain Parquet files with Glue Data Catalog
options.explanation: is incorrect. Plain Parquet has none of the transactional, time travel, or schema evolution features required — it is just a file format with no metadata layer.

options.text: Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog
options.explanation: is correct. Apache Iceberg and Apache Hudi are open table formats that add ACID transactions, time travel (query historical snapshots), schema evolution, and partition evolution on top of S3-stored Parquet files. Both are fully supported by AWS Glue, Athena, and EMR.

options.text: Amazon Redshift external tables via Redshift Spectrum
options.explanation: is incorrect. Redshift Spectrum is an S3 query layer that extends Redshift SQL to S3 data, not a table format with ACID or time travel capabilities.

options.text: AWS Lake Formation governed tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Lake Formation governed tables provide some transactional capabilities, but they use Lake Formation-specific APIs and are less portable than Apache Iceberg or Hudi.

questions.reference: [Using the Iceberg framework in AWS Glue - AWS GlueUsing the Iceberg framework in AWS Glue - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-format-iceberg.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon DynamoDB table stores user sessions with TTL set on each item. The table uses on-demand capacity mode. Queries filter on a non-key attribute (last_active_date). Performance is poor for these queries. What should you do?

options.text: Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for all read operations
options.explanation: is incorrect. DAX improves read latency for point lookups (GetItem, BatchGetItem) and caches query/scan results, but it does not fix the underlying scan caused by missing an index on `last_active_date`.

options.text: Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) with last_active_date as the partition key
options.explanation: is correct. DynamoDB requires queries to use the primary key or a secondary index. Filtering on a non-key attribute (`last_active_date`) requires a full table scan, which is slow and expensive. A Global Secondary Index (GSI) with `last_active_date` as the partition key allows efficient queries on that attribute without scanning the entire table.

options.text: Switch to provisioned capacity mode with auto-scaling
options.explanation: is incorrect. The capacity mode (on-demand vs. provisioned) affects how throughput is managed, not query patterns. Switching to provisioned does not add an index.

options.text: Use DynamoDB Streams to export data to Athena for analytical queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. DynamoDB Streams + Athena export is useful for analytical workloads on historical data, but it does not solve real-time query performance on the live DynamoDB table.

questions.reference: [Using Global Secondary Indexes in DynamoDB - Amazon DynamoDBUsing Global Secondary Indexes in DynamoDB - Amazon DynamoDB](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/GSI.html)

questions.stem: Which Amazon Redshift feature allows you to query data directly in S3 without loading it into Redshift, using the same SQL interface?

options.text: Redshift Data Sharing
options.explanation: is incorrect. Redshift Data Sharing allows sharing live data between Redshift clusters or Redshift Serverless namespaces within the same AWS account or across accounts — not querying S3.

options.text: Redshift Spectrum
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Redshift Spectrum extends Redshift's SQL querying capability to data stored in S3, using the Glue Data Catalog as the schema registry. Queries can join Spectrum external tables with Redshift local tables. Data does not need to be loaded — Spectrum pushes query predicates down to the S3 layer.

options.text: Redshift Serverless
options.explanation: is incorrect. Redshift Serverless is a deployment model for Redshift that eliminates cluster management, but it still requires data to be loaded (or uses Spectrum separately to query S3).

options.text: Redshift COPY command
options.explanation: is incorrect. The COPY command loads data from S3 into Redshift local tables — the opposite of querying S3 in place.

questions.reference: [Amazon Redshift Spectrum - Amazon RedshiftAmazon Redshift Spectrum - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c-using-spectrum.html)

questions.stem: You need a fully managed NoSQL database optimized for storing and querying semi-structured JSON documents with flexible schemas, supporting both document and key-value access patterns. Which AWS service is best suited?

options.text: Amazon DynamoDB
options.explanation: is incorrect. DynamoDB is a key-value and document database with limited query flexibility — it does not support rich document queries like MongoDB-style `$elemMatch`, aggregation pipelines, or complex nested document indexing.

options.text: Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility)
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon DocumentDB is a managed document database service compatible with MongoDB APIs, designed for storing, querying, and indexing JSON documents. It supports flexible schemas, rich document queries, and aggregation pipelines.

options.text: Amazon Aurora
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon Aurora is a relational (SQL) database engine (MySQL or PostgreSQL compatible) designed for structured tabular data, not flexible JSON document storage.

options.text: Amazon ElastiCache
options.explanation: is incorrect. ElastiCache is an in-memory cache (Redis or Memcached) designed for low-latency data access, not document storage and querying.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) - Amazon DocumentDBWhat is Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) - Amazon DocumentDB](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/documentdb/latest/developerguide/what-is.html)

questions.stem: You need to orchestrate a multi-step data pipeline that includes Glue jobs, Lambda functions, and EMR jobs with dependencies between steps. Failures should trigger retries. Which AWS service is designed for this?

options.text: Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules
options.explanation: is incorrect. EventBridge scheduled rules can trigger a single Lambda or target on a schedule, but they do not orchestrate multi-step pipelines with dependencies and retry logic.

options.text: AWS Step Functions
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Step Functions is a serverless workflow orchestration service that allows you to define state machines with sequential, parallel, and branching steps. It natively integrates with Glue, Lambda, EMR, DynamoDB, SageMaker, and more. Built-in retry logic, error handling, and visual workflow monitoring make it ideal for complex data pipelines.

options.text: Amazon SQS with Lambda triggers
options.explanation: is incorrect. SQS with Lambda triggers is useful for decoupled event-driven processing but does not provide pipeline orchestration with dependency management or step-level retry.

options.text: AWS Batch
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Batch manages batch compute jobs on EC2 or Fargate and can define job dependencies, but it is not designed for orchestrating heterogeneous AWS service calls (Glue + Lambda + EMR together).

questions.reference: [What is Step Functions? - AWS Step FunctionsWhat is Step Functions? - AWS Step Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/welcome.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job uses job bookmarks to track processed S3 files. After fixing a bug in the transformation logic, you need to reprocess all files from the beginning. What should you do?

options.text: Delete and recreate the Glue job
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deleting and recreating the Glue job is unnecessarily destructive — it would lose any configuration, triggers, and run history. Bookmark reset achieves the same goal without destroying the job.

options.text: Reset the Glue job bookmark to clear the bookmark state, then run the job
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue job bookmarks store the state of what has been processed so that subsequent runs only process new data. To reprocess all data, you reset the bookmark state via the Glue console (Actions > Reset job bookmark) or via the AWS CLI (`aws glue reset-job-bookmark`). After reset, the next job run processes all available data from the beginning.

options.text: Disable job bookmarks and re-enable them after reprocessing
options.explanation: is incorrect. Disabling job bookmarks would prevent the next run from tracking state, but the old bookmark state is not cleared — re-enabling would resume from the last bookmark position, not from the beginning.

options.text: Modify the job's MaxRetries setting to force full reprocessing
options.explanation: is incorrect. MaxRetries controls how many times a failed job run is automatically retried, not whether previously processed data is reprocessed.

questions.reference: [Tracking processed data using job bookmarks - AWS GlueTracking processed data using job bookmarks - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/monitor-continuations.html)

questions.stem: Your team runs multiple Apache Spark jobs on Amazon EMR for daily data processing. Jobs occasionally fail due to spot instance interruption. What should you configure to make the cluster resilient to spot interruptions at lowest cost?

options.text: Use only On-Demand instances for all EMR node types
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using only On-Demand instances eliminates Spot interruption risk but forgoes the significant cost savings that Spot instances provide for task nodes.

options.text: Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only
options.explanation: is correct. EMR best practice is to use On-Demand instances for master (cluster management) and core nodes (HDFS storage), which are critical for cluster stability. Task nodes only execute compute and have no HDFS data, so they are safe for Spot instances — if interrupted, running tasks are rescheduled to other nodes without data loss.

options.text: Use Spot instances for all nodes including master and core
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using Spot for core nodes risks data loss if HDFS block replicas on those nodes are lost when they are reclaimed. Using Spot for the master node risks complete cluster loss.

options.text: Enable EMR Serverless which automatically handles instance interruptions
options.explanation: is incorrect. EMR Serverless is a separate product that eliminates cluster management but has different pricing, limitations, and is not a simple toggle on an existing EMR cluster.

questions.reference: [Configuring Amazon EMR cluster instance types and best practices for Spot instances - Amazon EMRConfiguring Amazon EMR cluster instance types and best practices for Spot instances - Amazon EMR](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/ManagementGuide/emr-plan-instances-guidelines.html)

questions.stem: A daily ETL pipeline fails intermittently. CloudWatch Logs show the Glue job completing successfully, but downstream Athena queries return stale data. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Athena result caching is returning old query results from a previous execution
options.explanation: is incorrect. Athena result cache stores results per unique query string for up to 7 days, but this would only return stale results if the exact same query was run before. The pattern here — stale data after each daily job — points to a partition discovery issue, not caching.

options.text: The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE
options.explanation: is correct. When a Glue job writes new data to new S3 partitions, the Glue Data Catalog is not automatically updated unless explicitly configured. Athena queries the Glue catalog for partition metadata, so new partitions are invisible to Athena until discovered. Running a Glue Crawler (or executing `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` / `ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION` in Athena) updates the catalog with new partitions.

options.text: S3 eventual consistency is delaying object visibility to Athena
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon S3 has provided strong read-after-write consistency for all operations since December 2020. S3 eventual consistency is no longer a concern.

options.text: Glue DynamicFrames do not flush buffered writes to S3 immediately
options.explanation: is incorrect. Glue DynamicFrames flush all writes before the job completes — the Glue job completing successfully confirms the data is in S3. The issue is catalog metadata, not buffered writes.

questions.reference: [Using crawlers to populate the Data Catalog - AWS GlueUsing crawlers to populate the Data Catalog - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/add-crawler.html)

questions.stem: You need to monitor an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream and automatically scale the number of shards based on incoming data volume. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards
options.explanation: is correct. Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode automatically manages shard count based on incoming throughput, scaling up to handle traffic spikes and scaling down during low-throughput periods. This eliminates the need for manual shard management or custom auto-scaling code.

options.text: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the IncomingBytes metric and trigger a Lambda to call UpdateShardCount
options.explanation: is incorrect. The CloudWatch alarm + Lambda + UpdateShardCount approach works but adds significant operational complexity. On-Demand mode achieves the same goal automatically without custom code.

options.text: Use Kinesis Scaling Utilities (open-source) deployed on EC2
options.explanation: is incorrect. The open-source Kinesis Scaling Utilities are a community tool that adds operational burden of maintaining EC2 instances. AWS-native On-Demand mode is the recommended approach.

options.text: Kinesis shards are fixed and cannot be scaled after stream creation
options.explanation: is incorrect. Kinesis shards can absolutely be scaled — either via On-Demand mode (automatic) or manually via `UpdateShardCount` API in provisioned mode.

questions.reference: [Choose the right mode to stream in - Amazon Kinesis Data StreamsChoose the right mode to stream in - Amazon Kinesis Data Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/how-do-i-size-a-stream.html)

questions.stem: You need to create a CloudWatch dashboard that shows the number of records processed per minute by a Kinesis Data Stream, Glue job DPU utilization, and S3 PUT requests. What metrics must you configure?

options.text: All metrics are automatically available; create a CloudWatch dashboard and add them as widgets
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 request metrics and Glue detailed metrics are not enabled by default; they require explicit configuration.

options.text: Kinesis and S3 metrics are in CloudWatch by default; Glue job metrics require enabling Glue job observability metrics
options.explanation: is correct. Kinesis Data Streams publishes metrics (IncomingRecords, GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds, etc.) to CloudWatch automatically. S3 request metrics (PutRequests, GetRequests) must be enabled per bucket under S3 > Metrics > Request metrics — they are not enabled by default. AWS Glue DPU utilization metrics require enabling the Continuous Job Metrics or Observability Metrics feature in the Glue job configuration.

options.text: All three services require custom metric publishing via the CloudWatch Logs agent
options.explanation: is incorrect. None of these services require custom CloudWatch Logs agent publishing — their metrics are published natively to CloudWatch once enabled.

options.text: S3 request metrics must be enabled at the bucket level; Kinesis and Glue metrics are automatic
options.explanation: is incorrect. Option D is partially correct about S3 but incorrectly states that Glue metrics are automatic. Glue DPU utilization metrics require enabling observability metrics.

questions.reference: [Monitoring metrics with Amazon CloudWatch - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceMonitoring metrics with Amazon CloudWatch - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/cloudwatch-monitoring.html)

questions.stem: An S3 bucket stores sensitive customer PII data. Data engineers should be able to read and write objects, but must not be able to delete them. How do you enforce this using IAM?

options.text: Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject
options.explanation: is correct. In IAM, not granting an action is an implicit deny — which works when no other policy grants the permission. However, if another policy (e.g., an admin policy or a broader S3 policy attached to the role) grants `s3:DeleteObject`, the implicit deny is overridden. Adding an explicit Deny for `s3:DeleteObject` ensures the restriction holds regardless of other permissions — IAM explicit denies always override any allow.

options.text: Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject; omit s3:DeleteObject from the policy (implicit deny)
options.explanation: is incorrect. An implicit deny (simply omitting the action) is insufficient in environments where other policies or roles might grant broader S3 permissions. Explicit deny is the robust approach for critical data protection.

options.text: Enable S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode on the bucket
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 Object Lock prevents object deletion or overwriting for a defined retention period — it provides a different (stronger) form of protection but is a bucket-level data protection feature, not an IAM access control pattern.

options.text: Use an S3 bucket policy that allows only the data engineer IAM role
options.explanation: is incorrect. A bucket policy restricting to a specific IAM role controls who can access the bucket, but without an explicit deny on DeleteObject, an admin or a policy update could grant deletion access.

questions.reference: [Policy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access ManagementPolicy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access Management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html)

questions.stem: Your data lake contains multiple S3 buckets across accounts. You need a centralized service to catalog all datasets, manage access permissions, and enforce column-level security for sensitive columns. Which AWS service provides this?

options.text: AWS Glue Data Catalog with IAM resource policies
options.explanation: is incorrect. The Glue Data Catalog stores metadata (database/table/schema definitions) and supports resource-based IAM policies, but it does not natively enforce column-level security or centralized cross-account access management the way Lake Formation does.

options.text: AWS Lake Formation
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Lake Formation provides centralized data lake governance: it catalogs datasets (via Glue Catalog integration), manages table and column-level access permissions, applies row-level and cell-level security, and enforces permissions across Athena, Redshift Spectrum, Glue, and EMR. Column-level security is a key Lake Formation feature — you can grant access to specific columns of a table.

options.text: Amazon Macie
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon Macie is a security service that uses machine learning to discover and classify sensitive data (PII, credentials) in S3 — it provides discovery and alerting, not access control or column-level security.

options.text: AWS IAM Identity Center
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS IAM Identity Center provides single sign-on and centralized identity management, not data-level access control or column-level security for analytics services.

questions.reference: [What is AWS Lake Formation? - AWS Lake FormationWhat is AWS Lake Formation? - AWS Lake Formation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lake-formation/latest/dg/what-is-lake-formation.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job must read from an RDS database in a private VPC subnet (no public access). What configuration is required?

options.text: Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue Connections define network connectivity parameters (VPC ID, subnet ID, security group) for private data sources. When a Glue job references a Connection, it runs in an ENI (Elastic Network Interface) attached to the specified subnet, allowing direct private access to RDS in the same VPC. The security group must allow inbound connections from the Glue ENI's security group.

options.text: Create a VPC endpoint for AWS Glue in the RDS VPC
options.explanation: is incorrect. A VPC endpoint for AWS Glue allows Glue's control plane to communicate privately, but it does not enable the Glue job worker to reach RDS — the Glue Connection (with VPC/subnet) is needed for that.

options.text: Attach a NAT Gateway to the Glue job's network configuration
options.explanation: is incorrect. A NAT Gateway provides outbound internet access for private subnet resources, but the Glue job and RDS are in the same VPC — they should communicate via private IP without NAT.

options.text: Enable RDS public accessibility and restrict access with a security group rule for the Glue IP range
options.explanation: is incorrect. Enabling RDS public accessibility exposes the database to the internet, which is a security risk and unnecessary since both Glue and RDS are in the same VPC.

questions.reference: [AWS Glue connection properties - AWS GlueAWS Glue connection properties - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/connection-properties.html)

questions.stem: You need to ensure that sensitive data stored in S3 is automatically classified and that any unencrypted S3 buckets are detected and flagged for compliance. Which combination of AWS services covers both requirements?

options.text: Amazon Macie alone — it detects both sensitive data and unencrypted buckets
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon Macie is focused on sensitive data discovery and classification; while it reports on some bucket security posture findings, it is not the primary tool for encryption compliance monitoring across all buckets.

options.text: Amazon Macie for sensitive data classification and AWS Config for encryption compliance monitoring
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Macie uses machine learning to automatically discover and classify sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) in S3 buckets. AWS Config's `s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled` managed rule continuously evaluates whether S3 buckets enforce server-side encryption and flags non-compliant buckets. Using both provides comprehensive coverage: Macie for content-level sensitive data classification, AWS Config for encryption compliance.

options.text: AWS Security Hub alone — it aggregates all findings without needing separate services
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Security Hub aggregates and correlates findings from Macie, Config, GuardDuty, and other services, but it does not perform the underlying sensitive data discovery or encryption compliance checks — it relies on the individual services to produce those findings.

options.text: AWS Config alone — it can classify sensitive data content and check encryption settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Config evaluates resource configuration against compliance rules but does not inspect the content of S3 objects to classify sensitive data.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon Macie? - Amazon MacieWhat is Amazon Macie? - Amazon Macie](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html)

questions.stem: A Redshift cluster query logs contain SQL statements that include raw customer email addresses. You need to ensure that sensitive data is masked in query results. Which approach implements this?

options.text: Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) allows defining masking policies that substitute sensitive column values with redacted or tokenized values based on the user's role. Users without full access see masked values in query results — preventing raw PII from appearing in results or logs.

options.text: Encrypt the Redshift cluster at rest with KMS
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encryption at rest with KMS protects data stored on disk from physical access, but it does not affect what values appear in query results or audit logs.

options.text: Use Redshift column-level access control to restrict email column access
options.explanation: is incorrect. Column-level access control would block the column entirely, returning an access denied error — not masking it. Users who need to work with the data (but see masked values) would be blocked entirely.

options.text: Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption for Redshift audit logs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encrypting CloudWatch Logs for Redshift audit logs protects the log storage, but does not prevent raw PII from appearing in the logs in the first place.

questions.reference: [Dynamic data masking - Amazon RedshiftDynamic data masking - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/t_ddm.html)

questions.stem: You need to build a real-time data pipeline that reads from a Kinesis Data Stream, enriches each event by looking up reference data in DynamoDB, and writes results to S3. Which service provides the lowest operational overhead?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Kinesis Data Analytics (Managed Apache Flink) provides more sophisticated streaming capabilities (stateful processing, exactly-once semantics) but requires Flink application code and expertise, resulting in higher operational overhead.

options.text: AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Lambda-only approach with Kinesis trigger requires managing the Lambda function's fan-out delivery to S3 (writing custom S3 buffering/batching logic), making it more complex than Firehose for S3 delivery.

options.text: Amazon EMR Streaming with Spark Structured Streaming
options.explanation: is incorrect. EMR Streaming requires provisioning and managing an EMR cluster with Spark Structured Streaming, which is the highest operational overhead option.

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation
options.explanation: is correct. Kinesis Data Firehose with a Lambda transformation is the lowest-overhead approach: Firehose handles reading from Kinesis, buffering, retrying, and writing to S3; the Lambda function performs DynamoDB lookups for enrichment. No cluster management or Flink/Spark expertise is needed.

questions.reference: [Transform source data in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data FirehoseTransform source data in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/data-transformation.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift table query takes 30 minutes. EXPLAIN shows a step with DS_DIST_ALL_INNER, indicating one table is redistributed to all nodes for every join. The redistributed table has 10 million rows and is 500 MB. What is the recommended fix?

options.text: Set DISTSTYLE EVEN on the large redistributed table
options.explanation: is incorrect. DISTSTYLE EVEN distributes rows evenly across nodes by round-robin, which does not prevent redistribution during joins — the join key values will still need to be moved at query time.

options.text: Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently
options.explanation: is correct. `DS_DIST_ALL_INNER` means the inner table is redistributed to all nodes at query time. At 500 MB, this is within the practical limit for DISTSTYLE ALL, which replicates the table to every node permanently. This eliminates the runtime redistribution step entirely — each node always has a local copy for joins.

options.text: Increase the Redshift cluster node count
options.explanation: is incorrect. Adding more nodes increases total cluster capacity but does not eliminate the redistribution step; it just makes each redistribution slightly faster.

options.text: Enable Concurrency Scaling to handle the redistribution load
options.explanation: is incorrect. Concurrency Scaling adds additional cluster capacity for burst read queries but does not change the distribution strategy or eliminate redistribution overhead.

questions.reference: [Distribution styles - Amazon RedshiftDistribution styles - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_choosing_dist_sort.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement data quality checks on incoming S3 data before it is processed by Glue. Checks include: null count threshold, value range validation, and uniqueness. Which AWS service provides managed data quality rules for Glue data?

options.text: AWS Glue Data Quality (DQDL rules)
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue Data Quality uses the Data Quality Definition Language (DQDL) to define declarative quality rules (ColumnValues, IsUnique, RowCount, etc.). Rules can be applied to Glue tables or embedded in Glue ETL jobs as a pre/post-processing step. Results are stored as quality scores and can fail the job or trigger alerts. This is the native AWS approach for Glue-based data quality.

options.text: Amazon DataZone data quality rules
options.explanation: is incorrect. Amazon DataZone is a data management and governance service focused on data discovery, cataloging, and sharing across business domains — it does not provide Glue-integrated DQDL data quality rules.

options.text: Custom Lambda function with Great Expectations library
options.explanation: is incorrect. A custom Lambda with Great Expectations is a valid open-source approach, but it adds operational overhead of maintaining Lambda code and third-party library dependencies compared to the native Glue Data Quality feature.

options.text: AWS Config with custom rules for S3 data validation
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Config evaluates AWS resource configurations against compliance rules — it is not designed to validate the content of data files in S3.

questions.reference: [AWS Glue Data Quality - AWS GlueAWS Glue Data Quality - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/glue-data-quality.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue Spark job processes 10 TB of data daily. After optimizing the Spark code, average job duration is still 4 hours. The data is read from S3 in Parquet format with 400,000 small files (25 MB each). What single change would most reduce job duration?

options.text: Increase the number of DPUs to 200
options.explanation: is incorrect. Increasing DPUs does not help if the bottleneck is small file overhead — more workers just means more workers sitting idle waiting for task scheduling, not faster throughput.

options.text: Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes
options.explanation: is correct. 400,000 files of 25 MB each creates extreme small file overhead: Spark creates one task per file, causing massive task scheduling overhead and frequent S3 LIST/GET API calls. Compacting to approximately 20,000 files of 512 MB each reduces the task count by 20x, dramatically reducing scheduling overhead and S3 API calls. This is the primary bottleneck and the highest-impact single change.

options.text: Enable Glue Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust DPUs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Glue Auto Scaling dynamically adjusts DPUs based on workload, but it cannot fix the fundamental small file problem — it would still schedule 400K tasks.

options.text: Switch from Parquet to ORC format for better compression
options.explanation: is incorrect. Switching from Parquet to ORC changes the compression and encoding format but does not address the small file problem. Both Parquet and ORC perform poorly with 400K small files.

questions.reference: [Best practices design patterns: optimizing Amazon S3 performance - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceBest practices design patterns: optimizing Amazon S3 performance - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/optimizing-performance.html)

questions.stem: Your organization requires that all data in S3 used by the data lake be encrypted with customer-managed keys that you can rotate annually. Which encryption configuration satisfies this requirement?

options.text: Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3 (AES-256)
options.explanation: is incorrect. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys with no customer control over the key material or rotation timing — the keys are managed entirely by AWS.

options.text: Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK)
options.explanation: is correct. SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key (CMK) allows annual key rotation via AWS KMS key rotation policies or manual rotation. You retain control over the key — you can disable, revoke, or audit all usage in CloudTrail. This satisfies both the customer-managed key and annual rotation requirements.

options.text: Use client-side encryption with your own key before uploading to S3
options.explanation: is incorrect. Client-side encryption with your own key requires managing the key outside AWS, losing the KMS audit trail and key management infrastructure. It also adds significant operational complexity.

options.text: Enable S3 Block Public Access and encrypt using SSE-C
options.explanation: is incorrect. SSE-C (customer-provided keys) requires you to supply the encryption key with every S3 request — it does not use KMS and therefore has no KMS audit trail, rotation policy, or centralized key management. S3 Block Public Access is an unrelated access control setting.

questions.reference: [Using server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys (SSE-KMS) - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceUsing server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys (SSE-KMS) - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html)

questions.stem: Amazon Athena queries on a partitioned S3 table are scanning more data than expected, even though partition pruning should apply. The partition filter is applied using a WHERE clause. What could cause this?

options.text: The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning
options.explanation: is correct. Partition pruning in Athena (and most query engines) requires the filter to reference the partition column directly without wrapping it in a function. `YEAR(date_col) = 2024` applies a function to the partition column, preventing the engine from pushing the filter to partition metadata — it must scan all partitions and evaluate the function on each row. The fix is to use `date_col BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'`.

options.text: Athena does not support partition pruning
options.explanation: is incorrect. Athena fully supports partition pruning — it is one of the key performance features for partitioned S3 tables.

options.text: The S3 bucket versioning is enabled, causing Athena to scan all object versions
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 bucket versioning stores multiple versions of objects but does not cause Athena to scan all versions — Athena reads only the current (latest) version of each object.

options.text: The Glue Crawler set the wrong data type for the partition column
options.explanation: is incorrect. A wrong partition column data type might cause query errors or type cast issues, but it would not cause Athena to silently scan more partitions than expected.

questions.reference: [Partition your data - Amazon AthenaPartition your data - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/partitions.html)

questions.stem: You need to transfer 50 TB of data from an on-premises data center to Amazon S3 over a dedicated 10 Gbps AWS Direct Connect connection. Approximately how long will the transfer take at maximum throughput?

options.text: Approximately 11 hours
options.explanation: is correct. 50 TB = 50 × 1024 × 8 Gbits = 409,600 Gbits. At 10 Gbps: 409,600 / 10 = 40,960 seconds ≈ 11.4 hours at full theoretical throughput. In practice, usable throughput is 60–80% of nominal, so expect 14–18 hours. The exam tests the theoretical maximum calculation, which gives approximately 11 hours.

options.text: Approximately 2 days
options.explanation: is incorrect. Two days (48 hours) would correspond to roughly 2.4 Gbps average throughput — far below the 10 Gbps Direct Connect capacity.

options.text: Approximately 5 days
options.explanation: is incorrect. Five days would imply less than 1 Gbps average throughput, which is not representative of a dedicated 10 Gbps Direct Connect link.

options.text: AWS Direct Connect cannot be used for S3 data transfer
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Direct Connect is absolutely used for S3 data transfer via the S3 gateway endpoint or virtual private interface.

questions.reference: [What is Direct Connect? - AWS Direct ConnectWhat is Direct Connect? - AWS Direct Connect](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: A Step Functions state machine orchestrates a data pipeline. One state makes an API call to a third-party service that takes 20-30 minutes to complete. The state machine should wait for the result without polling. Which Step Functions pattern should you use?

options.text: Task state with a polling loop (Wait + Choice state)
options.explanation: is incorrect. A polling loop (Wait + Choice state that periodically checks job status) works but is inefficient — it consumes state transitions on every poll, increasing cost and adding latency.

options.text: Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete
options.explanation: is correct. The `.waitForTaskToken` pattern is designed for exactly this use case. Step Functions pauses the workflow and provides a task token to the worker (passed to the third-party API call). The external service performs its work and calls back to the Step Functions API (`SendTaskSuccess`/`SendTaskFailure`) with the token, resuming the workflow. The workflow can wait up to 1 year at no additional cost.

options.text: Parallel state with a 30-minute timeout
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Parallel state runs branches concurrently and waits for all to complete — it does not pause execution waiting for a callback. A 30-minute timeout would abort the state if the third-party service hasn't responded.

options.text: Express Workflow with synchronous execution
options.explanation: is incorrect. Express Workflows have a maximum duration of 5 minutes for synchronous execution — insufficient for a 20–30 minute third-party operation.

questions.reference: [Discover service integration patterns in Step Functions - AWS Step FunctionsDiscover service integration patterns in Step Functions - AWS Step Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/connect-to-resource.html)

questions.stem: You need to perform time travel queries on a Delta Lake table stored in S3 to recover data that was accidentally deleted. You are using Amazon Athena. What must be true to support this?

options.text: Athena does not support Delta Lake time travel; use Amazon EMR with Spark instead
options.explanation: is incorrect. Athena does support Delta Lake table queries including time travel, via the native Delta connector available in Athena's engine.

options.text: The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon Athena supports Apache Iceberg natively with time travel queries. For Delta Lake tables, Athena can also query historical versions using `VERSION AS OF <n>` or `TIMESTAMP AS OF '<ts>'` syntax. Delta Lake's transaction log (`_delta_log`) tracks all operations. The transaction log must be intact for time travel to work.

options.text: Enable S3 Versioning on the Delta table bucket and use the S3 console to restore previous file versions
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 Versioning stores multiple versions of individual S3 objects, but this is not the same as Delta Lake's table-level time travel semantics. Restoring individual S3 files via the S3 console would not correctly restore a consistent table snapshot.

options.text: Delta Lake time travel requires Databricks Runtime; it is not available on AWS
options.explanation: is incorrect. Delta Lake is an open-source format and is not limited to Databricks Runtime — AWS services including Glue, EMR, and Athena support Delta Lake tables.

questions.reference: [Query Linux Foundation Delta Lake tables - Amazon AthenaQuery Linux Foundation Delta Lake tables - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/delta-lake-tables.html)

questions.stem: You need to audit all API calls made to your S3 buckets and Glue Data Catalog for compliance purposes, including who accessed what data and when. Which AWS service provides this audit trail?

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch Logs
options.explanation: is incorrect. CloudWatch Logs collects application and infrastructure logs from services, but it does not automatically capture AWS API call audit trails the way CloudTrail does.

options.text: AWS CloudTrail
options.explanation: is correct. AWS CloudTrail records all AWS API calls — including S3 data events (GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject) and Glue Data Catalog API calls — with identity (IAM user/role), timestamp, source IP, and request parameters. CloudTrail logs can be stored in S3, analyzed with Athena, and used for compliance auditing across multiple services.

options.text: S3 Server Access Logs
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 Server Access Logs record S3 requests (with requestor, object, action, response) but only for S3 — they do not cover Glue Data Catalog API calls, and they are less structured than CloudTrail events.

options.text: AWS Config
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS Config tracks configuration changes to AWS resources over time (e.g., when a bucket policy changed), not the individual API access patterns required for data access auditing.

questions.reference: [What Is AWS CloudTrail? - AWS CloudTrailWhat Is AWS CloudTrail? - AWS CloudTrail](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-user-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to be notified when an AWS Glue job fails within 5 minutes of the failure. Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the glue.driver.jvm.heap.used metric with an SNS notification
options.explanation: is incorrect. The `glue.driver.jvm.heap.used` metric reflects JVM memory usage, not job failure state. A CloudWatch alarm on this metric would alert on memory pressure, not job failures.

options.text: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches Glue job state change events with FAILED status and triggers an SNS topic
options.explanation: is correct. AWS Glue publishes job state change events to Amazon EventBridge automatically. Creating an EventBridge rule with the pattern `{source: 'aws.glue', detail-type: 'Glue Job State Change', detail: {state: ['FAILED']}}` and targeting an SNS topic delivers near-immediate failure notifications. This is event-driven — no polling delay — and is the recommended AWS pattern for Glue job monitoring.

options.text: Enable Glue job email notifications in the Glue console notification settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no built-in Glue console email notification setting for job failures — EventBridge + SNS is the correct native approach.

options.text: Configure an S3 event notification on the Glue logs bucket
options.explanation: is incorrect. S3 event notifications trigger on object creation or deletion in S3 buckets — not on Glue job state changes. The Glue job log files being written to S3 would trigger on every log batch, not specifically on job failure.

questions.reference: [Amazon EventBridge](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge/latest/userguide/eb-service-event.html)

questions.stem: You need to migrate a 10 TB MySQL database from on-premises to Amazon Aurora MySQL with minimal downtime. The database serves an application that cannot tolerate more than 30 seconds of downtime during the final cutover. Which approach is best?

options.text: Stop the application, run mysqldump, transfer the dump to Aurora, restore, then start the application
options.explanation: is incorrect. `mysqldump` of a 10 TB database would require significant downtime (hours) while the dump runs, transfers, and restores — far exceeding the 30-second tolerance.

options.text: Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source
options.explanation: is correct. AWS DMS performs a full load of the existing data, then switches to CDC (reading MySQL binary logs) to replicate ongoing changes. The application continues running during migration. When Aurora is within seconds of the source's current position, you stop writes to the source, wait for DMS to apply the remaining changes, and cut over — all within seconds to minutes of downtime.

options.text: Use AWS DataSync to replicate MySQL binary log files to S3, then load into Aurora
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS DataSync is for file storage migration (copying files from NFS, HDFS, or S3) and does not support MySQL binary log replication to Aurora.

options.text: Create an Aurora Read Replica of the on-premises MySQL and promote it
options.explanation: is incorrect. Aurora read replicas cannot be created from on-premises MySQL databases — Aurora replication requires an Aurora source or DMS-based initial migration.

questions.reference: [What is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration ServiceWhat is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dms/latest/userguide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement an Amazon Redshift cluster that serves both interactive BI queries from analysts and bulk nightly ETL loads. Both workloads must not interfere with each other. Which Redshift feature isolates these workloads?

options.text: Redshift Query Monitoring Rules (QMR)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Query Monitoring Rules (QMR) define automated actions when queries exceed thresholds (e.g., cancel queries that exceed a time limit or memory usage), but they do not proactively isolate or allocate resources between workloads.

options.text: Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs
options.explanation: is correct. Redshift Workload Management (WLM) allows defining query queues with allocated memory percentages and concurrency slots. You create a BI queue with high concurrency and a query timeout, and an ETL queue with high memory allocation. Queries are routed to queues based on user groups or query groups. This isolates workloads on the same cluster.

options.text: Create separate Redshift clusters for BI and ETL
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating separate clusters completely isolates workloads but doubles the cost and requires data to be accessible from both clusters. WLM achieves isolation on a single cluster more cost-effectively.

options.text: Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling for BI queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Concurrency Scaling adds transient cluster capacity to handle burst read queries, but it does not prevent ETL jobs and BI queries from competing for resources on the main cluster.

questions.reference: [Workload management - Amazon RedshiftWorkload management - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_workload_mngmt_classification.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer's IAM role has s3:GetObject permission on a specific S3 bucket. An S3 bucket policy explicitly denies s3:GetObject for all principals. Can the data engineer read objects from this bucket?

options.text: Yes, the IAM role allow takes precedence over the bucket policy deny
options.explanation: is incorrect. IAM role allows do not take precedence over explicit denies in bucket policies — explicit deny always wins in AWS policy evaluation.

options.text: No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies
options.explanation: is correct. AWS IAM policy evaluation follows a strict hierarchy: explicit denies always override allows, regardless of which policy contains them. If the S3 bucket policy explicitly denies `s3:GetObject` for all principals (including the data engineer's role), the explicit deny cannot be overridden by an IAM role allow. The data engineer cannot access the bucket.

options.text: Yes, if the data engineer is the bucket owner
options.explanation: is incorrect. Even the bucket owner's IAM principals are subject to explicit denies in a bucket policy, unless the deny condition explicitly excludes the account root or specific principals.

options.text: It depends on whether the IAM role has an attached permissions boundary
options.explanation: is incorrect. A permissions boundary limits the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have, but it does not change the fundamental rule that explicit denies override allows. Even without a permissions boundary, the explicit bucket policy deny blocks access.

questions.reference: [Policy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access ManagementPolicy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access Management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement data lineage tracking for your AWS data lake, showing how data flows from source S3 buckets through Glue ETL jobs to final Redshift tables. Which AWS service provides native lineage tracking?

options.text: AWS CloudTrail with Athena analysis
options.explanation: is incorrect. CloudTrail captures AWS API calls and can be analyzed with Athena, but this provides an audit log of API activity, not a data lineage graph showing how data transforms and flows between systems.

options.text: Amazon DataZone
options.explanation: is correct. Amazon DataZone provides data lineage visualization showing how data flows through your AWS data estate. It integrates with Glue, Redshift, Athena, and S3 to automatically capture and display lineage graphs.

options.text: AWS Glue Data Catalog with lineage enabled
options.explanation: is incorrect. The AWS Glue Data Catalog stores metadata (database/table/schema definitions) but does not natively visualize or track data lineage graphs showing data flow between sources, transformations, and targets.

options.text: AWS Glue with Apache Atlas integration
options.explanation: is incorrect. Apache Atlas is an open-source data governance platform that can be integrated with Glue, but this requires additional infrastructure and is not a native AWS managed service.

questions.reference: [What is Amazon DataZone? - Amazon DataZoneWhat is Amazon DataZone? - Amazon DataZone](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/datazone/latest/userguide/what-is-datazone.html)
